sentences.text: So on we go in the United States.

I'm living in Whitestone, part of Flushing, New York, and in this little town where people were not aware of refugees, wars, and I don't believe there were any Jewish people living in this town as well.
A very close knit place, and one morning very soon after arriving, my uncle took me to school.
Now, you must understand, my older cousin there, a female who was 14, cheerfully put on makeup and pretended she didn't know me.
My other cousin, unfortunately-- the younger one-- was in bed with rheumatic fever and was not able-- she had to be very quiet, and she really wasn't available for a whole lot.
But a wonderful wardrobe was there, and my aunt used to iron things by the hour and hang them into her closet.
And very carefully, every so often, I would ask my aunt whether I could wear one of those skirts or one of those shoes to school and was always told, no, that was impossible.
And so therefore, off I went to school in long knit brown stockings, and high shoes that were laced beyond the ankle, and some very typical European and old fashioned looking clothes.
And really felt like somebody from outer space, perhaps.
I don't think they looked like that, though.
And my uncle took me to school, left me in the principal's office to register my attendance and so forth.
And of course, I was given a test.
I couldn't speak English.
I can't imagine how I understood the things that were being said to me.
I think my uncle did a little bit of translation.
He was also fluent in French so that we were able to leave the German end very much out of the picture.
And language, there was no issue.
I didn't have it.

The principal in vain tried math, and in European math, divisions are done with the two little dots instead of the-- the little house that we use here in America with the larger number and the smaller number on either side.
And when they drew this little box for me with the divisor and things in it, I automatically-- I was good in math in Germany and in Belgium-- I automatically assumed it was square root.
And since I learned square root in Brussels, I proceeded to do a square root whereupon they were totally baffled in elementary school and pronounced that I didn't know math.
And so it was decreed I was going to go into the first grade.
And as I came into the first grade, the teacher made me sit on the dunce's chair, put the dunce's hat on.
I had no idea what this meant.
And took out her long pointer, and pointed to me.
And said to her children, this is what a European looks like.
I didn't understand her, but I knew it wasn't very friendly.
The kids all giggled.
They were all first graders, and they thought it was extremely funny.
And there I was, lost, distressed, teary.
To face the world by myself, really, in every way.
After class-- there was one little black girl in the class, which was astounding because there were no black people in this town.
And it turned out that she was the daughter from a servant at a very well-to-do household.
And to this day, I don't know why she was allowed to go to the public school, but it was liberal enough so that she could go.
And she became my friend.
She sat with me on the curb, and helped me learn English, and helped me with my homework.
And when she got through with me, I sounded as though I had just come from Georgia.
I had the most fascinating southern accent, and of course, now everybody is again making fun of me.
But somehow I didn't-- it didn't matter, and she was a good friend.
And I went to the principal about four weeks into the struggle and said to him that I wanted to strike a deal with him.

That I would do the work in the grade that I was in, but that he would permit me to give me all the books and all the material pertaining to the next grade, and that I would study that along with my other material.
And when I was ready, I would come to him, and he would give me an exam.
And if I passed that exam, he had to promote me.
And must reluctantly, he did, thinking tongue in cheek that this was kind of a strange scheme.
But he-- he entered into it, and this is how I brought myself up to level, is I studied twice as hard and read ahead.
Of course, I lost a lot.
I mean, I couldn't just concentrate on what was being taught.
American history made absolutely no sense to me because I had no beginning, so to speak.
But I memorized my material, and I knew-- I thought I knew what I was doing, and so I brought myself up to grade in that manner.

After a while, then my-- I was transferred to the city, to my parents, who had then-- there was a family meeting and my grandparents, who had been living with my mother's sister, had now decided that they were going to live with us.
And the sister and her husband moved out to New Jersey, and my parents and my grandparents took an apartment in Astoria, New York, and this is where we were going to continue living.
And--
Very, very little.
When my father came out of the concentration camp, although raised in an orthodox fashion, he said there was no God and never referred to God again in all of our traveling.
When we got to New York, my mother's family was not observant at all, and so there was no emphasis on religion in the house.

However, my own outlook-- I suppose in adult years you would call it philosophy-- but my own outlook on life was such that I felt very grateful to God for having gotten me to the point where we were together and in good health.

And I always, from then on and still do, feel very strongly that I survived in order to-- to live a Jewish life, to raise children in the Jewish faith, and thus be part of procreation and populating a small corner of the world with Jewish adults and children, that that was somewhat of a mission.
I also felt it was an obligation to those of my family who perished, both my mother-- both my grandmother at the age of 89 and my aunt, Clementine, and her husband were deported.
My aunt and uncle went to [INAUDIBLE], which is in Poland, to a camp, and were lost, annihilated, there.
My grandmother was taken on a stretcher to Theresienstadt and perished there.
My young cousin, that I spent all my growing up years with, was deported and also was lost in the camp.
And so I always felt a very strong obligation to their memory, to carry on Jewish faith, which I do to this day.

And so when my mother's-- in the family, my mother's family, there really wasn't all that much emphasis on religion, and I always had terrible arguments with my father who persistently said there is no God.
And I said I want to go to synagogue, and he said you go do what you want.
I think you're foolish.
And he did this until even when I was an adult and I had children later.
He nixed it all the way, and I fought him all the way, and said, no, it's not true, because look-- and I trotted out all these things.
And He never spoke about his experience in Buchenwald, but I knew that that was the reason why he had turned away from God.
Not his religion, but God.
And so now, we're in New York City.
We're in Washington Heights where a lot of the refugees-- in fact, for a while there it was dubbed Little Germany, only the Jewish kind, up at 178th Street in New York City.
And again, I started school, and at that school, one of the first things that I remember is I learned my multiplication tables and my addition in German.
And to this day in my head, when I do-- when I do multiplication and adding a column of numbers, I do it in German.
Somehow or other I must've slipped in math class, and the teacher who used to stalk the aisles and terrify the girls came upon me and said, what did you say?
And I tried to fudge it over, but he realized I was doing it in another language.
And he called me up to the head of the class, and he made me do it out loud on the blackboard.
And he embarrassed me to no end in front of the classroom, because literally, I'm not as quick and adept as doing it in English.
It just seems to pop into my head.
And that's how I started my next school experience, which was then junior high school, which was not a very comfortable feeling at the time.
However, in the seventh grade, I met a young girl my own age.
We met in the corridor on the day that my mother had major surgery, and she couldn't speak English, either, very well.
She was Czechoslovakian.
She was not Jewish.
In fact, she came from a very strict Catholic home.
And somehow, she must have asked me something that set me off, and I began to cry because I was under pressure and worry.
We consoled each other, and we've been friends ever since.
She comes to visit us here.
We talk regularly on the phone, and we continue to be very good friends.
So that friendship started, and that sort of eased the pain a bit, because she was having similar experiences, and we sort of leaned on each other.
And it made-- made the hard road a little easier.
And then at that point in my life, we had to earn money.
My father had then-- my mother was artistically inclined.
She was trained in graphic arts.
She always wanted to teach nursery school children arts and crafts, and she had taken job in a shower curtain factory or shop, whatever-- however you want to call it.
And she was painting designs on plastic shower curtains.
You know, lovely, big, free things.
I was left at home to do the housework, because in truth, she was tired.
She was over-- well, she was 45 years old, 48 years old, and working all day.
She came home-- Her relationship with her mother was not very good, and so I was sort of plunked right in the middle.
And I shopped for my grandparents and did as much housework as I could.
And so that was my responsibility, so to speak.
And my father, in the meantime, the younger brother who was sent to this country to open up a branch of the textile company, which my maternal grandfather was also in that business.
And that is how my mother and father had initially met, that the two bosses had a meeting, and there was my mother as a young girl, and she was introduced to my father.
And so, this younger brother of my mother's, who had come in 1932, had established a firm, a textile firm.
And recognizing that my father was very knowledgeable, that this had been his business, his factory, and he had contacts in Belgium and in France, took him into the business to manage.
A lot of it was physical work.
He-- he had some very strong and stout black workers loading trucks and things, and here was my-- my skinny, little father pushing these people around and directing things.
But he was very happy working for my uncle.
My uncle was very, very good to him and in many, many ways, and he worked for him until he became sick in 1960.
So he was there for almost 20 years that he-- well, not quite 20 years. 15, 17 years, in his chosen field doing what he knew to do.
In fact, on one occasion in 1949 he-- my uncle sent him to Europe on a buying trip, at which point he made contact with his old colleagues in Belgium and Holland and so forth.
And also on that trip to Europe, he met his older brother who was his partner in business, who was then living in France, in Paris.

And together, they set up a monument on the Jewish cemetery for the members of the family that were deported, and lost, and legally sold the property for which a post-war they-- with Germany being in rubble, it was just pittance, but they didn't want anything to do with-- with Germany.
They just sold it for a few thousand dollars and thought that that was pretty nifty.
And so he worked for my uncle, and that was a very, very good arrangement.
And I went to plod on through school, still dreaming perhaps someday to do something with medicine.
I knew I couldn't afford a medical school, but I always thought, well, there must be a branch or something that I'm going to be able to afford.
So I saved money fastidiously for babysitting jobs, like $0.25 a week for sitting every night, doing dishes, and ironing, and all kinds of things for my babies.
And also, putting a little money-- a little of my money went to the family to help support, to pay for the combined expenses of the family, and I did anything.
I swept out stores.
I remember working after hours in a card shop on Fifth Avenue where I replaced the cards that needed to be restocked.
And wherever there was money to be made, there I went to see what I could contribute and what I could save in order to further my education.

As I became more informed, I realized that in New York the system was if you wanted to take an academic program in college preparation, there were, of course, the sciences, and the languages, and the math that had to be taken.
And then being fully aware that I could never just go to college, that I would have to work my way through, I took secretarial courses as well.
Doubled my-- doubled up my program, and learned stenography and typing so that I could have a job, secretarial job, and go to night college was the general idea what I was sort of striving for.
And it wasn't easy.
I couldn't squeeze it all in because from the age of almost 16, I worked for the New York telephone company on their night shift from 3:00 to 11:00, 3:00 in the afternoon to 11:00 at night.
Wonderful pay.
I had no time for lunch.
I did my classes, took no time for lunch, had a sandwich, jumped on the subway, went downtown to Canal Street, and clocked in at 3 o'clock on my job.
And worked on a long distance board, which was the rest of my interesting education.
Of all the things that I heard over the wires, the way people talked to me.
And I had to learn American geography in a flash because there were no computers then.
We worked on trunks.
We plugged in.

And there were no direct connections, so if somebody wanted to call in the middle of the country, we used to have to hopscotch from New York to Ohio to-- to some other stop in order to get to Arizona.
And you had to be pretty fast at it.
Otherwise the company docked you for minutes.
[DRINKING]
So I was a very busy person I had no time for extracurricular activities or social-- anything social at all.
It was all-- I was hell bent on education, so to speak.
And again, made contracts with the principal.

I needed my languages, and so I told the principal I would take one year of French, attend classes, and then I wanted to take the three-year Regent Exam in French, which of course was bluffing, and the four-year German Regents Exam for college entrance after taking a year of classroom attendance.
And it was a furious battle.
My grammar was atrocious.
But I wrote magnificent translations and fluent papers where you had to tell stories or relate stories and things.
And I remember the principal standing at the door of my German class and fighting with the German teacher.
And he said, no, he would not pass her.
My-- my grammar was atrocious, and the principal saying, have you ever in all your teaching career had a translation like this or a paper like this?
And in the end, the principal won out, and I got my credits for both the French and the German, which was a big help because I didn't have room in my program for this kind of thing.
You know, there was no fooling around.
I needed every moment, every possible moment.
And that's how I battled my way through the American educational system from the word go.
Some of my friends who were a little older and managed to get into places like Julia Richmond school in New York.
They were eased a lot of higher education because the teachers there knew their way around, but my provincial teachers had no idea and were of absolutely no help.

In fact, one of the German teachers that I had-- no, actually she was an English teacher-- insisted that I had an accent and a lisp, and forced me to go into elocution classes, which at our high school were broadcasting.
We broadcast in front of a microphone and read things on the microphone, did funny plays in front of the microphone.

And she did me an enormous favor by banishing me to that, because my dict-on together with the New York telephone company-- my diction and my expression was a lot of things have stood me in very good stead that it was very helpful as I went along my way.
And in the meantime, we were living with my maternal grandparents.
My grandfather was diabetic and not well at all, and while we were living together, he had a major stroke, and was paralyzed, and was not able to give himself the insulin injections anymore.
So here my desire in the medical area sort of came through because I was elected to do the insulin injections with him.
My grandmother wasn't able to do it.
She couldn't bring herself to doing it in there twice a day.
I was his assistant in giving him these injections.
Also, at the same time, my brother was diagnosed with grand mal and had seizures, bad seizures, at unpredictable times.
And invariably, since my mother worked and my father worked, I was the one that always had to come to the rescue.
I learned how to keep his mouth open, and how to put his tongue forward, and make sure that he wouldn't choke on his tongue.
And make excuses if it happened on the sidewalk to people, and tell him that he was OK.
And sort of carry on in many areas.
And I learned a lot, a great deal.
My current family.
Well, the love of my life, Henry.
We met after the war.
We were introduced by my cousin who was in the service with him overseas during the war with General Patton.
Introduced us, and we were married in 1948.
The most wonderful day in my life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I feel that every day from 1938 on was a gift that we had for life.
From that point on, I felt every major event in our lives, Henry and mine, were a beautiful present of children, three children, and our three grandchildren.
Just special beyond words.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
[COUGHING] We're very fortunate, because living here in Newton, all three of our children and our grandchildren are in New England, very close by.
Our little granddaughter, who is going to be 11, is really within walking distance of our house.
And we manage to speak practically every day, and we have a very special bond.
Our daughter lives in Hanover, New Hampshire.
That is in Norwich, Vermont, across the river from there.
Her husband works at Mary Hitchcock Hospital.
He's a physician.
And she teaches at Dartmouth.
And her two children are also very special to me.
They're older, and I feel we have a very close relationship.
We're always together for the holidays.
Sometimes with us, and since we've moved to the small apartment, Brenda does the holidays in her home, and our younger son is also living in Newton now.
For a while, he was in Rhode Island.
So they're really very close, and we're a very close knit family, which is what makes us supremely happy.
We need to stop.
We're going to change a tape.
OK.
